s: A full Catholic church hidden across three attic floors
w: A schuilkerk -- concealed church -- built across three attic floors of a canal house in the 1660s. Catholics barred from public worship since the 1578 Reformation packed into this tiny nave for a century. The street facade gives nothing away. One of the last intact hidden churches in the country.
_facts: Built in the 1660s across the top three floors of a canal house at Oudezijds Voorburgwal 40
Catholics could not worship publicly after the 1578 Reformation; schuilkerken were the workaround
Opened as a museum on 28 April 1888
One of the best-preserved examples of a hidden church in Amsterdam
Receives about 85,000 visitors per year

s: Medieval city gate, anatomy theatre, and scene of Rembrandt's masterpiece
w: Amsterdam's oldest non-religious building began as the Sint Antoniespoort city gate in 1488. When the walls expanded it became a weigh house, then guildhall shared between surgeons and painters. The surgeons used its Theatrum Anatomicum for public dissections -- the one Rembrandt recorded in 1632. Now a cafe.
_facts: Built as Sint Antoniespoort (city gate) in 1488
Later served as weigh house, guildhall, anatomical theatre, museum and fire station
Oldest non-religious building in Amsterdam
Rembrandt's 1632 painting 'The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp' depicts surgeons of the Waag
Listed as rijksmonument (national monument) in 1970

s: One coffee plant here fathered all of South America's coffee culture
w: Founded in 1638 as a herb garden for doctors, one of the world's oldest botanical gardens. VOC traders filled it: a single coffee plant that arrived here in the late 17th century became the parent of the entire coffee industry of Central and South America. Two Mauritian oil palms seeded all of Southeast Asia.
_facts: Founded in 1638 by Amsterdam City Council as the Hortus Medicus for physicians and apothecaries
A single coffee plant in the collection was the parent for all coffee culture in Central and South America
Two oil palms brought from Mauritius seeded plantations across Southeast Asia
One of the world's oldest botanical gardens, located in the Plantage district
Plants and seeds brought by VOC traders formed the core 17th-century collection

s: A grand riding school hidden behind an ordinary-looking canal-house facade
w: From Vondelstraat it looks like an ordinary Amsterdam brick building. Step through and the space opens into a soaring neo-classical riding hall modelled on the Spanish Riding School in Vienna, built in 1882. Horses still exercise beneath the ornate ironwork gallery. Oldest riding school in the Netherlands, est. 1744.
_facts: Oldest riding school in the Netherlands, dating to 1744
Current building constructed in 1882, inspired by the Spanish Riding School in Vienna
Designed to be hidden behind the street facade typical of Amsterdam canal houses
Listed as a rijksmonument (national monument)
Owned by Stadsherstel Amsterdam, a non-profit restoring historic monuments

s: 210,000 film cans and a white spaceship across the IJ ferry
w: Take the free ferry from behind Centraal Station -- three minutes across the IJ -- to a tilting white building by Vienna architects. Inside: 57,000 film titles, 210,000 cans of acetate and four cinemas. The archive traces back to 1946, when the founders of Kriterion decided the Netherlands needed to preserve its films.
_facts: Opened 4 April 2012, designed by Delugan Meissl Associated Architects (Vienna)
Predecessor was the Dutch Historical Film Archive, founded in 1946 by Kriterion's founders
Collection includes 57,000 film titles, 210,000 cans of acetate film, 700,000 photographs
Located in Overhoeks neighbourhood, accessible by free IJ ferry from Centraal Station
Houses four cinemas: 300, 127, 127 and 67 seats

s: The world's first museum dedicated entirely to microbes
w: The world's first museum dedicated to microorganisms, opened 2014 inside Artis Zoo. The collection is alive -- cultures of bacteria, fungi, archaea and viruses doing things in real time. The Kiss-O-Meter measures microbes swapped in a single kiss. The argument: microbes run the biosphere; we merely live in it.
_facts: Opened 30 September 2014 as part of Artis Zoo; claims to be the world's first microbe museum
Exhibits include living microbes: cultures of bacteria, fungi, archaea and viruses
The Kiss-O-Meter measures the number of microbes exchanged in a single kiss
Located at Plantage Kerklaan 38-40
Core premise: to promote a positive view of microbes as essential to life

s: A Golden Bend canal house devoted entirely to art about cats
w: In 1990, Bob Meijer opened this museum in memory of his cat John Pierpont Morgan -- named after the banker. The canal house at Herengracht 497 holds Picassos, Rembrandts and Toulouse-Lautrecs, all depicting cats. It sits in the Golden Bend of the Herengracht. The owner still lives upstairs. Cats still on duty.
_facts: Founded in 1990 by Bob Meijer in memory of his red tomcat John Pierpont Morgan
Houses cat-themed works by Picasso, Rembrandt, Toulouse-Lautrec, and Corneille
Located at Herengracht 497, built in 1667 for patrician brothers Willem and Adriaen van Loon
John Adams visited the house during his time as US ambassador to the Dutch republic
Museum owner lives on the second floor; live cats reside in the building

s: No electricity since 1675 -- a thousand candles light the Sephardic synagogue
w: Completed in 1675 for Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain and Portugal, the Esnoga has never been wired for electricity. Two chandeliers holding 1,000 candles illuminate the sanctuary on festival evenings. The floor is covered in fine sand -- the only surviving sand-floored synagogue outside the Caribbean.
_facts: Completed in 1675 for Amsterdam's Sephardic Jewish community
No internal electric system; 1,000 candles in two chandeliers when lit
Sand floor -- the only surviving sand-floored synagogue outside the Caribbean
Torah ark carved from a single piece of jacaranda wood from Brazil, dating to 1744
Sephardim expelled from Spain in 1492, then Portugal in 1496, settled in Amsterdam

s: Rembrandt buried as a pauper here; his bones were later removed and destroyed
w: Built 1620-1631, the largest Protestant church in the Netherlands. Rembrandt van Rijn was buried somewhere under its floor on 8 October 1669 -- broke, his grave number lost. After twenty years the church cleared his remains, standard practice for the poor. A 1909 plaque marks the approximate spot.
_facts: Built 1620-1631, designed by Hendrick de Keyser, the largest Protestant church in the Netherlands
Rembrandt buried here on 8 October 1669 as a poor man; exact grave number was lost
His remains were removed and destroyed after 20 years (customary for the poor)
1909 memorial marker on north wall based on a model from the Nachtwacht
Hendrickje Stoffels and Titus van Rijn also buried here, equally unmarked

s: Amsterdam's Enlightenment concert hall -- later the Dutch Communist HQ
w: 'Happy through merit.' Founded 1776, this neoclassical building hosted Brahms, both Schumanns, and the Dutch premieres of Beethoven's Ninth and the Symphonie fantastique. After the society dissolved in 1888, printers moved in. After 1945, the Dutch Communist Party made it their HQ until crowds stormed it in 1956.
_facts: Founded in 1776 for five departments: Music, Drawing, Physics, Commerce and Literature
Building opened 31 October 1788, designed by Jacob Otten Husly
Hosted Brahms, Saint-Saens, both Schumanns; Dutch premieres of Beethoven's Ninth and Symphonie fantastique
The Concertgebouw's small hall is a replica of the Felix Meritis oval concert hall
After 1945 became Dutch Communist Party HQ; stormed by crowds on 4 November 1956 over Hungary invasion

s: The stock exchange that taught Amsterdam modernism how to build
w: H.P. Berlage's red-brick exchange of 1903 replaced a building the city called 'the mausoleum.' It became the most influential building in early Dutch modernism, steering a generation from historicist ornament toward functionalism. The Amsterdam School absorbed its lessons. The Dutch royal wedding was held here in 2002.
_facts: Designed by Hendrik Petrus Berlage, constructed 1896-1903
Built to replace the Zocher Exchange of 1848, nicknamed 'the mausoleum'
Considered a founding influence on Dutch modernism, functionalism and the Amsterdam School
On 2 February 2002 it hosted the civil ceremony of the wedding of King Willem-Alexander and Maxima
Now used as concert, exhibition and conference venue

s: A tobacco company's gift that became the epicentre of the Provo revolution
w: The bronze urchin on the Spui was a gift from a cigarette company in 1960. When artist Robert Jasper Grootveld discovered its origins in 1964, he staged midnight anti-smoking happenings around it, arriving in costume, chanting and dancing. The Provo movement formed at these gatherings and invented the white bicycle.
_facts: Bronze statue donated to Amsterdam by a cigarette company in 1960
From 1964, artist Robert Jasper Grootveld held anti-smoking happenings around it at midnight on Saturdays
Gatherings attracted the Provo counterculture movement which held weekly meetings at the statue
Provo pioneered the White Bicycle Plan and other anarchist civic proposals
Located on the Spui square

s: Amsterdam's 1885 gasworks, scrubbed clean and handed to the artists
w: Built in 1885 as the city's largest gas-extraction plant, between the Haarlemmer canal and the first railway in the Netherlands. The city bought it in 1898; the great iron Gashouder was added in 1902. Gas production stopped in 1967. The Victorian-industrial buildings are now theatres, studios and cultural venues.
_facts: Built in 1885 by the Imperial Continental Gas Association
Was the largest gas extraction plant in the Netherlands when opened
The big Gasholder was built in 1902
Gas production stopped permanently in 1967 when natural gas was found in Groningen
Now a cultural venue complex with theatres, studios and the Gashouder events space

s: Cut the Cullinan diamond -- and rebuilt after Nazis killed 96% of their workers
w: In 1908, Joseph Asscher struck the Cullinan -- largest diamond found -- and split it on the second blow after his blade broke on the first. During WWII, Nazis deported the family and all 500 polishers; over 96 percent were murdered. Fifteen polishers and ten family members survived. They rebuilt at the same address.
_facts: Founded 1854; headquarters at Tolstraat 127, Amsterdam, since founding
In 1908 Joseph Asscher cleaved the Cullinan diamond (3,106 carats) for King Edward VII
Also cut the 997-carat Excelsior diamond in 1903
In WWII, Nazis deported the Jewish family and all 500 master polishers; over 96% of polishers were murdered
Ten Asscher family members and fifteen polishers survived; rebuilt the company postwar

s: Six stone heads, a canal house, and 25,000 hermetic manuscripts inside
w: Built in 1622 with six carved stone busts -- Apollo, Ceres, Mercury, Minerva, Bacchus, Diana -- on the facade. Legend says they are six burglars beheaded by the maid. The building now houses the Embassy of the Free Mind: 25,000 Hermetic, alchemical and Rosicrucian manuscripts, opened in 2017 by Dan Brown.
_facts: Built in 1622 for stock trader Nicolaes Sohier; facade attributed to Hendrick de Keyser
Six heads represent Roman gods Apollo, Ceres, Mercury, Minerva, Bacchus and Diana (legend says beheaded burglars)
The Embassy of the Free Mind opened here in October 2017, inaugurated by Dan Brown
Houses the Ritman Library: 25,000 Hermetic, alchemical and Rosicrucian manuscripts since 1958
John Adams visited the house during his time as US ambassador to the Dutch republic

s: The VOC's 1656 arsenal, with an 18th-century ship replica moored outside
w: Designed by Daniël Stalpaert and built in 1656 as the admiralty's naval warehouse, storing rope, canvas, tar and cannon for the Amsterdam fleet. A replica of the VOC vessel Amsterdam is moored outside. The map collection includes Blaeu family charts and the first printed description of Magellan's circumnavigation.
_facts: Built in 1656 as 's Lands Zeemagazijn (naval storehouse), designed by Daniël Stalpaert
The museum moved into this building in 1973
Moored outside is a replica of the Amsterdam, an 18th-century VOC ship, built 1985-1990
Map collection includes works by 17th-century cartographers Willem and Joan Blaeu
Has a copy of the first edition of De Moluccis Insulis, the first account of Magellan's circumnavigation

s: A photography museum in a canal house that was once a private art vault
w: Keizersgracht 609 was willed by art collector Carel Fodor as an exhibition space; Museum Fodor ran here 1863-1994. Since 2001 it has been Foam: four simultaneous photography shows -- documentary, fashion, art, experiment. Cartier-Bresson, Avedon and Newton have all had retrospectives here.
_facts: Building at Keizersgracht 609-611, acquired by art collector Carel Joseph Fodor (1801-1860)
Museum Fodor operated here 1863-1994; Netherlands Design Institute 1994-2001
Foam Fotografiemuseum opened in 2001
Runs four simultaneous exhibitions in documentary, art and fashion photography
Has hosted major retrospectives of Henri Cartier-Bresson, Richard Avedon, and Helmut Newton

s: They preserve a layer of dust on the walls because cleaning it changes the sound
w: Opened in 1888 on 2,186 wooden piles; by 1983 the piles were rotting and the walls had cracked. The pilings were replaced with concrete. The Main Hall's 2.2-second reverberation makes it ideal for Mahler. Dust on certain surfaces is left intact to preserve acoustics. Led Zeppelin played here.
_facts: Opened 11 April 1888, designed by Adolf Leonard van Gendt, inspired by the Gewandhaus in Leipzig
Built on 2,186 wooden piles; by 1983 piles were rotting and walls had cracked; replaced with concrete in 1985
Main Hall reverberation time of 2.2 seconds with audience, ideal for late Romantic repertoire
Dust in several places is deliberately left to preserve acoustics
Led Zeppelin, Pink Floyd and The Who performed in the hall in the 1960s

s: The last of 26 corn mills that once sat on Amsterdam's city walls
w: Twenty-six corn mills once stood on the bastions of Amsterdam's city walls; De Gooyer is the last. At 26.6 metres, the tallest wooden mill in the Netherlands. Named around 1609, it moved three times as the city expanded, settling at Funenkade in 1814. The blades still turn but grind nothing.
_facts: Built in the 16th century; moved several times as Amsterdam expanded
Last of the 26 corn mills that stood on the bastions of Amsterdam's 17th-century walls
At 26.6 metres, the tallest wooden mill in the Netherlands
Named around 1609 after owners Claes and Jan Willemsz from Gooiland
Reached its present location on Funenkade in 1814; registered as a national monument

s: Twenty-five centuries of smoking pipes, from clay Dutch to carved meerschaum
w: Over 2,000 pipes spanning 25 centuries in a canal house on the Prinsengracht -- clay Dutch church-wardens, Japanese kiseru, carved meerschaum, hand-painted German porcelain bowls. When Groningen's Niemeyer tobacco museum closed in 2011, this collection absorbed hundreds more pieces. The national reference collection.
_facts: Founded as a private collection in 1969; opened publicly in 1975; moved to Amsterdam in 1995
Located at Prinsengracht 488, between Leidseplein and the Rijksmuseum
Permanent exhibition displays over 2,000 items representing 25 centuries of smoking pipes
Holds the national reference collection for smoking pipes and tobacco in the Netherlands
When Niemeyer tobacco museum in Groningen closed in 2011, acquired some 400 additional items

s: The workers' church of the Jordaan, where the 1941 February Strike was planned
w: Hendrick de Keyser's most experimental church: Greek-cross plan, octagonal core, built 1620-1623 for the working-class Jordaan. In 1941, organisers of the February Strike against Nazi Jewish deportations held their first clandestine meetings on the Noordermarkt outside. A plaque on the south wall marks it.
_facts: Completed 1620-1623, designed by Hendrick de Keyser
Unique Greek-cross plan with octagonal core -- designed for the working-class Jordaan
Deliberately less grand than the Westerkerk, which served the middle and upper classes
Organisers of the February Strike of 1941 held their first public meetings on Noordermarkt square outside
Rijksmonument since 1970

s: Amsterdam's oldest building -- 60,000 dead beneath its floor, red lights outside
w: A wooden chapel stood here around 1213; the stone church consecrated in 1306. Amsterdam's oldest building: largest medieval wooden vault in Europe, Estonian oak from 1390. Under the floor: 2,500 gravestones, 60,000 dead. On 9 March at 8:39 am, sunlight falls on Rembrandt's wife Saskia's tomb. Outside: De Wallen.
_facts: Wooden chapel founded around 1213; stone church consecrated in 1306 as Roman Catholic, became Reformed in 1578
Ceiling is the largest medieval wooden vault in Europe; Estonian oak planks dated to 1390
2,500 gravestones in the floor covering 60,000 citizens buried here until 1865
Rembrandt's wife Saskia van Uylenburgh buried here; every 9 March at 8:39 am, sunlight illuminates her tombstone
Rembrandt's children were all christened here; the church is surrounded by De Wallen red-light district
